paper_title: ReDiffDet: Rotation-equivariant Diffusion Model for Oriented Object Detection

core_story.problem: Ori- ented objects are usually exhibited in arbitrary directions, densely arranged, and with extreme aspect ratios, which make it difficult to accurately predict locations and cate- gories.
core_story.limitation: Introduction Oriented object detection aims to locate targeted objects by a set of oriented boxes and categorize them [37].
core_story.insight: We observe that fitting the distribution of positions and sizes with Gaussian distribu- tions may not be optimal.
core_story.proposed_method: To overcome these limitations, we propose ReDiffDet, a frame- work that formulates oriented object detection as a rotation- equivariant denoising diffusion process.
core_story.experimental_evidence: The exten- sive experiments demonstrate ReDiffDet achieves promising performance and significantly outperforms the diffusion- based baseline detector.
core_story.summary: Ori- ented objects are usually exhibited in arbitrary directions, densely arranged, and with extreme aspect ratios, which make it difficult to accurately predict locations and cate- gories. Introduction Oriented object detection aims to locate targeted objects by a set of oriented boxes and categorize them [37]. We observe that fitting the distribution of positions and sizes with Gaussian distribu- tions may not be optimal. To overcome these limitations, we propose ReDiffDet, a frame- work that formulates oriented object detection as a rotation- equivariant denoising diffusion process. The exten- sive experiments demonstrate ReDiffDet achieves promising performance and significantly outperforms the diffusion- based baseline detector.

1. Introduction
Oriented object detection aims to locate targeted objects
by a set of oriented boxes and categorize them [37]. Ori-
ented objects are usually exhibited in arbitrary directions,
densely arranged, and with extreme aspect ratios, which
make it difficult to accurately predict locations and cate-
gories. To predict objects accurately, oriented object detec-
tors based on convolutional neural networks (CNN) have
emerged and made considerable progress, mainly including
two-stage [14, 39] and one-stage [21, 40] method. These
methods rely on empirically designed candidates, such as
region proposals [14, 39], anchor boxes [18, 40], and refer-

Third, we propose an aligned decoder for alignment between oriented boxes and image features.
In this paper, to alleviate the above issues, we propose a rotation-equivariant diffusion model for oriented object de- tection called ReDiffDet.
In addition, we propose a conditional encoder to obtain conditional boxes for conditioning.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms the baseline and achieves fa- vorable performance compared to traditional detectors.
Al- though our method demonstrates the rotation-equivariance of the reverse process, the properties of the other sam- pling strategy warrant further discussion.

In general, the main contributions of this paper are summarized in four folds: • As far as we know, this paper proposes the first rotation- equivariant diffusion model for oriented object detection called ReDiffDet for the orientation of boxes. • The conditional encoder is designed to produce condi- tional boxes, helping to prevent the random distribution of boxes across images. • The aligned decoder is designed to align oriented boxes with image features by wrapping interesting region fea- tures according to the specified boxes. • Experiments on five common datasets demonstrate that ReDiffDet is competitive with previously well- established detectors.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms the baseline and achieves fa- vorable performance compared to traditional detectors.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
The diffusion model has been successfully applied to var-
ious detection tasks. However, it still faces several chal-
lenges when used for oriented object detection: objects
that are arbitrarily rotated require the diffusion model to
encode their orientation information; uncontrollable ran-
dom boxes inaccurately locate objects with dense arrange-
ments and extreme aspect ratios; oriented boxes result in
the misalignment between them and image features. To
overcome these limitations, we propose ReDiffDet, a frame-
work that formulates oriented object detection as a rotation-
equivariant denoising diffusion process. First, we repre-
sent an oriented box as a 2D Gaussian distribution, form-
ing the basis of the denoising paradigm. The reverse pro-
cess can be proven to be rotation-equivariant within this
representation and model framework. Second, we design
a conditional encoder with conditional boxes to prevent
boxes from being randomly placed across the entire im-
age. Third, we propose an aligned decoder for alignment
between oriented boxes and image features. The exten-
sive experiments demonstrate ReDiffDet achieves promising
performance and significantly outperforms the diffusion-
based baseline detector. Codes are available at https:
//github.com/wokaikaixinxin/ReDiffDet.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Oriented object detection aims to locate targeted objects
by a set of oriented boxes and categorize them [37]. Ori-
ented objects are usually exhibited in arbitrary directions,
densely arranged, and with extreme aspect ratios, which
make it difficult to accurately predict locations and cate-
gories. To predict objects accurately, oriented object detec-
tors based on convolutional neural networks (CNN) have
emerged and made considerable progress, mainly including
two-stage [14, 39] and one-stage [21, 40] method. These
methods rely on empirically designed candidates, such as
region proposals [14, 39], anchor boxes [18, 40], and refer-

ence points [21, 22]. End-to-end transformers [19, 49, 52]
have attracted great attention by using a fixed set of learn-
able queries to eliminate the hand-designed components [2].
To void the surrogate of queries, DiffusionDet [4] first
adopts a diffusion model [34] for horizontal object detec-
tion [24] and directly detects objects from a set of random
boxes. It treats detection as a generative task [17] over the
space of sizes and positions of boxes. In training, Diffu-
sionDet [4] adds noise to ground truth boxes to obtain noisy
boxes and learns to predict boxes without noise. In infer-
ence, it denoises random boxes to get targets. We further
analyze the potential limitations of diffusion-based detec-
tors through two sets of studies in the following sections.
We analyzed the distribution of box characteristics, in-
cluding center coordinates, widths, heights, angles, and as-
pect ratios, as shown in Fig. 2. We observe that fitting the
distribution of positions and sizes with Gaussian distribu-
tions may not be optimal. Only the distribution of center
coordinates is more bell-shaped. Different datasets exhibit
significant variations in data characteristics. Diffusion-
Det [4] considers the distribution of coordinates, widths,
and heights separately, as multiple 1D distribution. But ori-

ented boxes are geometrically 2D oriented rectangles in a
2D plane. In addition, we conducted a small analytical ex-
periment with the condition that the center coordinates of
random boxes be confined to real boxes at the start of infer-
ence, as shown in Fig. 2. The accuracy is improved with the
condition, particularly when using a small number of boxes
and steps. We found that uncontrollable boxes introduce
randomness, but conditioning can help mitigate its effects.
This condition prevents the boxes from being randomly lo-
cated throughout the entire image.
According to the above observations, directly extending
the diffusion model into oriented object detection still suf-
fers from the following issues. 1) Equivariance: Objects
distributed with arbitrary orientations require the detector
to encode orientation information effectively. Ideally, the
detector should be able to consistently predict regardless
of the orientation of objects. 2) Conditioning: Random
boxes do not effectively cover arbitrarily oriented objects
or match the real size of objects with extreme aspect ra-
tios and scales. 3) Misalignment: Objects rotate arbitrarily,
while image feature maps are axis aligned. Complex back-
ground information will be extracted without alignment and
key semantic information will be lost.
In this paper, to alleviate the above issues, we propose a
rotation-equivariant diffusion model for oriented object de-
tection called ReDiffDet. ReDiffDet casts oriented object
detection as a generative task over the space of the Gaus-
sian distribution of boxes. In the training stage, standard
Gaussian noise is added to the distribution of ground truth
boxes. ReDiffDet is trained to predict the distribution of
target boxes from standard Gaussian distribution. In infer-
ence, ReDiffDet generates oriented boxes using the reverse
process. We theoretically prove the rotation-equivariance in
the reverse process under the Gaussian representation and
model framework. In addition, we propose a conditional
encoder to obtain conditional boxes for conditioning. Fi-
nally, we design an aligned decoder to align oriented boxes
and image features. In general, the main contributions of
this paper are summarized in four folds:
• As far as we know, this paper proposes the first rotation-
equivariant diffusion model for oriented object detection
called ReDiffDet for the orientation of boxes.
• The conditional encoder is designed to produce condi-
tional boxes, helping to prevent the random distribution
of boxes across images.
• The aligned decoder is designed to align oriented boxes
with image features by wrapping interesting region fea-
tures according to the specified boxes.
• Experiments on five common datasets demonstrate
that ReDiffDet is competitive with previously well-
established detectors.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this work, we are the first to explore the rotation-
equivariant diffusion model for oriented object detection by
formulating the detection as a denoising diffusion process,
transforming noise into a distribution of boxes. ReDiffDet
has several key properties, including equivariance, condi-
tioning, and alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate
that our method outperforms the baseline and achieves fa-
vorable performance compared to traditional detectors. Al-
though our method demonstrates the rotation-equivariance
of the reverse process, the properties of the other sam-
pling strategy warrant further discussion. Various geometric
shapes, such as 3D boxes, ellipses, and spherical boxes, can
be converted into Gaussian or other distributions. They can
also be modeled as diffusion processes using our approach.
